data.title: Great Elm Capital: Dramatic Downside Presents Opportunity To Accumulate (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Business development companies continue to feel the weight of the higher interest rate environment, and Great Elm Capital (NASDAQ:GECC) is no exception. When I previously covered GECC, I issued a hold rating due to the CLO exposure to below-investment-grade-rated securities. Now that the share price has experienced a drastic decline and GECC reported earnings, I wanted to revisit this BDC to provide some updated insights into its performance and outlook for the remainder of 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that GECC's share price has declined by about 19.6%. Most of the price drop has happened over the last week, and I believe that it is overdone. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of about 7.4% over the same time frame. GECC now offers investors an enticing dividend yield of 20.26% at this time. This high yield makes GECC an attractive choice for investors seeking a high dividend yield.

Fortunately, I believe that the price decline is a bit overdone. While there are certainly some signs of weakness within GECC's portfolio, I believe that the BDC currently offers an attractive value proposition for investors. For instance, GECC now has some portfolio companies in non-accrual status, and there is some exposure to a large business that filed for bankruptcy. These challenges have caused GECC to trade at a deep discount to NAV valuation, which may present an attractive opportunity to accumulate shares. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that GECC implements to generate its earnings.

Portfolio Strategy

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, GECC has investments at a total fair value of $217.4M that is spread across a diverse range of debt investments. GECC's portfolio contains 54 different debt investments and 15 equity investments. What I like most about the portfolio is that the majority of investments are structured as first lien loans, which sit at the top of the corporate capital structure and have the highest priority for repayment. This can act as a defensive measure for GECC and protect against scenarios where a portfolio company defaults and needs to liquidate assets. However, GECC is unique because it utilizes a different range of debt types, including the below:

  • First Lien: 47%
  • CLO (collateralized loan obligations): 17%
  • Specialty Finance: 11%
  • Second Lien: 7%
  • Unsecured: 4%
  • Yielding Equity: 3%
  • Other: 11%

Expanding on this diversity, GECC maintains an efficient allocation across different industries. As we can see below, GECC is most heavily allocated to Technology investments at 18% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Consumer Products and Chemical investments, which both account for 11% of the portfolio value respectively. However, I remain cautious about the health of the portfolio as the impact of President Trump's tariffs becomes more relevant.

Additionally, 73% of the portfolio is structured on a floating rate basis. This has allowed GECC to capitalize on the higher interest rate environment. A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher level of interest income that can be collected from borrowers. However, a higher interest rate can also put pressure on portfolio companies that are operating with thinner margins or that may be more reliant on debt.

Valuation & Risk

Since GECC is structured as a business development company, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, GECC now trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 33.06%. For reference, GECC has traded at an average discount to NAV of 15.35% over the last five-year period. To better conceptualize the current discount pricing in relation to its historical range, I added the red line to the graph below. As we can see, GECC now trades at one of the deepest discount levels over the last five years, which may be an indication that it is a good time to add shares.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $10.75 per share, which indicates a potential upside of nearly 44.3% from its current level. However, I don't believe this sort of growth will happen until interest rates are significantly lower. The Fed did implement a smaller 25 basis point cut to rates in September, but I don't think this is large enough to drive any growth. On a positive note, GECC has been able to keep its NAV within a consistent range over the last twelve months. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q2 2024: $12.06 per share
  • Q3 2024: $12.04 per share
  • Q4 2024: $11.79 per share
  • Q1 2025: $11.46 per share
  • Q2 2025: $12.10 per share

However, the recent share price decline may be related to some underlying deterioration of the portfolio. The latest earnings call, management confirmed that non-accruals amounted to nearly ~3% of fair portfolio value. Non-accruals primarily consisted of two portfolio companies, Maverick Gaming and Del Monte. Since the earnings call, there have been some additional developments that may have contributed to the decline in share price. There was a recent report that highlighted how First Brands, an automotive supplies business, filed for chapter 11 bankruptcy.

However, First Brands only had a 0.9% weight on GECC's total portfolio value. Therefore, I believe that the downside reaction is dramatic and presents an opportunity. Apparently, GECC had some exposure to First Brands, as well as other BDCs including:

  • Prospect Capital (PSEC): 0.3% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Palmer Square (PSBD): 0.19% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

Q2 Earnings

GECC reported its Q2 earnings at the beginning of August, and the results were solid. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.51 per share. This was the highest net investment income per share amount over the last twelve months. Total investment income amounted to $14.2M, which was a sizeable improvement from the prior year's total investment income of $9.5M. The amount of interest income received remains relatively flat, so the increase can be mostly attributed to an increase in dividend and other income.

However, net operating expenses did slightly increase over the last year. Net operating expenses amounted to $8.3M, which was higher than the prior year's net operating expenses of $6.4M. Most of the increase in expenses can be attributed to higher incentive fees.

From a balance sheet perspective, there are certainly some improvements that can be made. For instance, GECC currently sits at a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.44x, which indicates that GECC is more reliant on debt to fuel its operational growth. While the debt-to-equity ratio has declined from the prior quarter's ratio of 1.57x, this is still relatively high amongst BDC peers. Additionally, GECC currently has total debt outstanding at $201.4M, while the cash and equivalents sit low at $4.4M. Going forward, I would like to see a significant improvement in GECC's cash position because it would improve the BDC's ability to invest into new opportunities.

During the quarter, GECC was able to allocate $36.4M towards new investments. Of this total, $23.9M was allocated to one debt investment, while the remaining amount was allocated to one equity investment. I believe that GECC's cash balance needs to be substantially improved before the BDC takes on more debt to fuel additional growth.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.37 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 20.26%. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.51 per share. This indicates that the earnings support the distributions at a coverage rate of about 138%. Therefore, I have no concerns about the overall sustainability of the distributions at this time. However, it should also be mentioned that the distributions were cut a few years ago to offset the lower earnings.

While GECC has provided some supplementals in the past, I don't believe that investors should expect any additional supplementals to be paid out until macroeconomic conditions improve. However, management was able to confirm their optimism around the portfolio's ability to continue supporting the distributions.

We are excited for the second half of the year as we look to execute on our growth and optimization initiatives. We believe we remain well positioned to cover our dividend in 2025, continuing to deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns for our shareholders. -Matthew Dov Kaplan, CEO

Lastly, it's worth mentioning that the distributions received from GECC are fueled by net investment income, so they will be classified as ordinary dividend income. Ordinary dividends have the greatest tax burden for investors, which may not be appealing for investors looking to utilize GECC within a taxable brokerage account. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in GECC is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on GECC to a buy due to the attractive discount to NAV valuation. I believe that the downside movement is a dramatic reaction to some weakness within the portfolio. The non-accruals still remain relatively low, and despite the exposure to an investment that is going through a bankruptcy, these short-term challenges can be solved through new investment activity and future interest rate cuts. Despite the attractive valuation, investors should still be aware of the risks. For instance, GECC's debt-to-equity ratio remains high and the cash on hand is low.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FFTY: Spectacular Returns In 2025 Mask Issues
data.description:
Today, I would like to reassess the Innovator IBD® 50 ETF (NYSEARCA:FFTY), a passively managed exchange-traded fund with a concentrated portfolio and ultrahigh turnover, which I covered four times in 2022–2023, with the previous analysis published in June 2023.

And there is a good reason to restart coverage—FFTY is having such a moment in 2025, so unless something truly catastrophic happens, it will massively outperform the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) and even the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) for the year. This is extremely rare: incepted in 2015, the previous time FFTY beat IVV was in 2017.

I was always skeptical about FFTY in the past. Consistent underperformance, high expense ratio, and ultrahigh turnover were the main reasons for that. So should its recent success prompt me to move past skepticism and finally upgrade FFTY to a Buy? Let us address the main issues shortly here.

  • First, I would like to see consistent alpha, not a burst of outperformance.
  • Second, some healthy asset flows would be nice to see as well. However, although there is some growth this year, FFTY's AUM of $110.3 million is still a fraction of its September 2018 zenith, when it surpassed $570 million.
  • Third, its burdensome expense ratio of 0.80% has not gone anywhere.
  • Fourth, the very essence of FFTY's high-turnover strategy has not changed.

So while I appreciate that FFTY has masterfully leveraged the market ebullience this year and has beaten the S&P 500 ETF YTD, thus having a very high chance to deliver its second alpha year in more than a ten-year-long history, the conservative Hold rating is maintained.

Basics Of The Innovator IBD® 50 ETF

  • Inception: April 8, 2015
  • The Investment Adviser: Innovator Capital Management, LLC
  • The Investment Sub-Adviser: Penserra Capital Management LLC
  • AUM: $110.3 million, as of October 9
  • Net expense ratio: 0.80%
  • FFTY's benchmark as per the factsheet: the S&P 500 index

FFTY Strategy

So what is the essence of FFTY's strategy? First of all, as we know from its website, its core is the IBD® 50 Index. Importantly, incepted in April 2015, before November 20, 2017, it was actively managed. However, there is no reason to ignore its performance delivered prior to its transition to the passive approach. As we know from page 8 of the prospectus:

Prior to November 20, 2017, the Fund was actively managed using a quantitative strategy substantially similar to the methodology of the Index.

How Is The Index Calibrated?

The IBD® 50 Index has a highly sophisticated creation process notable for the extremely demanding criteria.

In short, as described on page 2 of the prospectus available on the FFTY website, the process starts with "7,000 equity securities that trade on the New York Stock Exchange, The Nasdaq Stock Market, LLC or NYSE American LLC." In the first step, it is assessed whether these names "meet certain baseline criteria," including "minimum stock price, minimum trading volume, minimum upward price movement, and minimum simple price moving average." Those that fail this test are filtered out, while those remaining are then screened for "undesirable" price actions to make the list even shorter.

Next, companies face a much more complicated test when they are assessed using 11 factors. The first five that are shown below form the base score.

  • Earnings Per Share Rating
  • Relative Price Strength Rating
  • Sales Margin Return on Equity Rating
  • Accumulation Distribution Rating
  • Industry Group Relative Strength Rating

What the remaining six do is that they "add to or subtract from the base score to derive the final aggregate score."

  • Growth in quarterly earnings per share and next quarter consensus estimates
  • 3-to-5-year annual earnings per share growth rate and next fiscal year's consensus estimates
  • Quarterly sales growth and next quarter consensus estimates
  • Acceleration in quarterly sales or earnings growth rates
  • Liquidity measured by share price and 50-day average daily trading volume
  • Annual return on equity metrics from the company's income statement

So 50 securities with the highest composite score, which amalgamates the above 11 factors, join the index. As to weighting, it has a complex scheme that depends on how significant a company's composite score is.

FFTY's Approach Is Quasi-Active

So in my previous articles, I called its strategy 'quasi-active.' The reason here is that, on the one hand, there is no doubt that it is passively managed. On the other hand, owing to the weekly reconstitutions, its turnover is 1,304%. And this is not just a lot; this is an immense figure.

Using the Seeking Alpha ETF Screener, I have checked whether there are any ETFs in the U.S. Equity class (including those actively managed) with a higher turnover. Interestingly, in this group encompassing 932 ETFs, only the Innovator IBD® Breakout Opportunities ETF (BOUT) has that figure above FFTY's at 1,448%.

So, while being an index-tracking ETF, FFTY adjusts its portfolio so deeply and so frequently that its turnover dwarfs those of active vehicles. Why does it matter? It is excellently explained in the prospectus:

A higher portfolio turnover will cause the Fund to incur additional transaction costs and may result in higher taxes when Shares are held in a taxable account. These costs, which are not reflected in Total Annual Fund Operating Expenses or in the example, may affect the Fund’s performance.

FFTY Performance: Startling 2025 Masks Years Of Dismal Returns

This year, FFTY has finally hit its stride, beating IVV and QQQ by a solid margin. But delving deeper, we see that there are a lot of issues that mar this outperformance story.

First, since April 9, 2015, FFTY has horribly underperformed IVV, as it beat this S&P 500 ETF only in 2017 and YTD.

Period FFTY IVV
May–December 2015 -6.77% -0.63%
2016 8.93% 12.15%
2017 37.63% 21.76%
2018 -16.76% -4.47%
2019 25.74% 31.25%
2020 18.20% 18.40%
2021 11.92% 28.76%
2022 -52.40% -18.16%
2023 12.40% 26.32%
2024 18.38% 24.93%
January–September 2025 30.68% 14.75%

Data from Portfolio Visualizer

So the median annual active return for the full nine calendar years it has in the books (2016–2024) was -6.55%, as per my calculations, which is certainly not a healthy result for an ETF with such a sophisticated strategy.

Looking at the annualized data (May 2015–September 2025), we also see a lackluster picture, especially considering that FFTY's maximum drawdown was 2.45x that of IVV and its downside capture was almost 154%.

FFTY Portfolio

An important remark worth making before we delve into all the nuances of FFTY's portfolio is that, as we discussed above, this is a quasi-active vehicle with a 1,304% turnover. That is to say, equities we currently see in the basket may be totally replaced during the next weekly reconstitution, and the factor story will morph into something new. With this in mind, let us dig in.

As of October 8, there were 50 equities in the FFTY portfolio, with the main ten companies having around 33% weight and IREN Limited being the main holding with a 4.45% weight.

Stock Weight in FFTY, as of October 8 Weight in IVV, as of October 7 Revenue FWD Sector
IREN Limited (IREN) 4.45% N/A 135.38% Information Technology
Stoke Therapeutics, Inc. (STOK) 3.93% N/A 43.47% Health Care
Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) 3.73% N/A 54.58% Industrials
Astera Labs, Inc. (ALAB) 3.70% N/A N/A Information Technology
Innodata Inc. (INOD) 3.61% N/A N/A Industrials
Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD) 3.54% 0.20% 38.04% Financials
Symbotic Inc. (SYM) 3.42% N/A 31.93% Industrials
Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) 3.34% 0.72% 36.16% Information Technology
Comfort Systems USA, Inc. (FIX) 3.32% N/A 20.60% Industrials
American Superconductor Corporation (AMSC) 3.24% N/A 27.75% Industrials

Created by the author using data from FFTY, IVV, and Seeking Alpha

Looking at their forward revenue growth rates, we see how considerable FFTY's exposure to the growth factor is. This in part explains its record gains this year.

Interestingly, while benchmarked against the S&P 500 index, FFTY has a rather small overlap with IVV, 19.7% (nine stocks), as per my calculations. In the S&P 500 ETF, these holdings have a 12.51% weight.

Sector Exposures

FFTY is not supposed to be sector-neutral, so it is reasonable to expect exposures that are materially different from IVV's. For instance, FFTY currently completely ignores consumer staples, energy, real estate, and utilities.

Besides, it is considerably overweight in health care, industrials, and materials while underweight in communication and consumer discretionary, among others.

FFTY Factor Mix

What is the factor story? In essence, I would characterize FFTY's current portfolio as unapologetically pro-growth, pro-momentum, value-agnostic, and high-beta-oriented (a weighted average 24-month beta at almost 2 is rather telling).

It is notable that FFTY is quite light in the trillion-dollar companies, with just 2.44% allocated. Quality characteristics are not perfect, but saying that they are totally inadequate would be unfair, as a 73.1% allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher is fine, but I still expect no less than 80% from ETFs heavy in mega caps.

Final Thoughts

All in all, while FFTY's performance has been spectacular this year, I believe what we are seeing now is something of a 2017 moment for it. In other words, this is a period of stupendous gains that do not translate into consistent, multi-year alpha. I might be very wrong with this assumption. Nevertheless, if history is any guide, the odds are not in favor of it outperforming the market for two years in a row. So I maintain the Hold rating.

data.title: Electronic Arts Acquisition Is 2nd-Largest Video Game Deal On Record
data.description:
An investor group's $56.78 billion deal to take Electronic Arts Inc. (EA) private would be the second-largest deal on record for the video game industry, solidly besting Take-Two Interactive Software Inc.'s (TTWO) purchase of Zynga Inc. but well behind Microsoft Corp.'s (MSFT) acquisition of Activision Blizzard Inc.

The transaction will be funded by a combination of cash from each of Silver Lake Technology Management LLC, Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, and Jared Kushner's A Fin Management LLC, as well as a roll-over of the Public Investment Fund's existing stake in EA, constituting an equity investment of approximately $36 billion. The deal also includes $20 billion of debt financing fully and solely committed by JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A., $18 billion of which is expected to be funded at close.

The EA deal is only slightly behind the Activision deal in terms of implied enterprise value divided by trailing 12-month revenue at 7.38x. However, it solidly outpaces the average 3.8x multiple for interactive home entertainment deals announced so far in 2025 for which we have available data.

The multiple is a sign that the buyers have a high degree of confidence in EA's revenue generation potential. In addition, we would expect significant cost-cutting measures, as the deal has a 27.51x implied enterprise value/trailing EBITDA multiple.

The company's core business revolves around releasing two sports titles, EA FC (formerly FIFA) and Madden, on an annual basis. Each is sold as a stand-alone piece of software retailing for $70, but they also drive significant revenue through their Ultimate Team modes.

These in-game purchases allow players to assemble their own squads out of randomized "packs" resembling trading cards. Outside of sports simulation, the company has also seen success with The Sims, Apex Legends and Star Wars Jedi in recent years.

However, the deal finds EA at a crossroads. Its sports-focused model ran out of momentum in 2024, with the company reporting its first decline in revenue since 2015.

Management is hoping to return to topline growth by reviving the Battlefield series. Battlefield 6 is scheduled to release Oct. 10, with a free-to-play battle royale to follow later that month. The game is being overseen by Vince Zampella, who is seen as one of the key creative forces in Activision's Call of Duty and who went on to develop Apex Legends for EA.

Battlefield is aiming for the transformational growth arc that Call of Duty benefited from when it released its own free-to-play battle royale mode in 2020, and the deal takes some pressure off EA to make an immediate impact.

A poor reception at launch would have pressured the stock price, forcing EA into a fight-or-flight position that could have negatively impacted long-term development. Now that investors are shored up with a pending deal, EA may have some cover to let the game grow and evolve organically.

Testing regulatory waters

In the wake of the Activision Blizzard deal announcement in 2021, we expected further consolidation in the console and PC gaming space. And while a handful of deals followed, including Sony Group Corp.'s (SONY) $3.70 billion acquisition of Destiny developer Bungie Inc., there was nothing quite on the same level.

We suspect the chilly regulatory reception to Microsoft, which already had a large market share in gaming, kept other tech-adjacent suitors on the sidelines. While the investor group in the EA deal does not wield as much market share as Microsoft, Saudi Arabia brings with it some exposure to the gaming space.

The Public Investment Fund purchased mobile developer Scopely Inc. for $4.90 billion through its Savvy Games Group division in 2023, and Scopely would go on to purchase the games business of Niantic for $3.50 billion in March 2025.

And in addition to Saudi Arabia's existing public holding in Electronic Arts of 9.91% of shares outstanding valued at $4.79 billion as of Sept. 29, the Public Investment Fund also holds significant stakes in Nintendo Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:NTDOY), Take-Two, NEXON Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:NEXOF) and Capcom Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:CCOEY).

The inclusion of Kushner, who is President Trump's son-in-law, may give the parties involved some confidence that the deal will be approved in the US, but other territories in which EA does significant business will want to weigh in as well.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Minutes Of ECB's September Meeting Add More Color To The 'Good Place'
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

Here are some of the most relevant passages from the minutes:

  • The ECB failed to specifically name Spain in its reference to exceptional performance, as well as sounding more confident about eurozone resilience. “Although the euro area economy had in the second quarter been more resilient than expected, this was largely due to the exceptional performance of the euro area’s fourth-largest economy. Other economies were not performing well, as also reflected in the visible softening of their respective labour markets.”
  • The ECB increasingly doubts the drop in the household savings rate. “The projections were based on a decline in the household saving rate. The robustness of this assumption was questioned, given that uncertainty about the economy was not expected to fade any time soon, and it was normal for households, in such conditions, to save more for precautionary reasons.”
  • There are growing concerns that Europe can live up to the 'global euro moment.' “While this message deserved the full support of the Governing Council, concerns were expressed that there was still insufficient drive for structural reform. In this context, it was observed that, according to the European Policy Innovation Council, only 11% of the Draghi report’s proposals had been fully implemented one year on.”
  • Even though there is no official ‘balance of risk’ for inflation, the minutes state that "risks surrounding the inflation outlook were identified on both sides."
  • Apparently, there are still more concerns about inflation undershooting than overshooting, as the minutes state that “several members viewed inflation risks as tilted to the downside,” while “a few members viewed inflation risks as tilted to the upside.”
  • Keeping rates on hold as a means to tackle uncertainty. “The environment remained more uncertain than usual, especially because of the still volatile global trade policy environment, but also owing to geopolitical developments. Such uncertainty could also justify keeping interest rates unchanged. In particular, the current situation is likely to change materially at some point, but it is currently difficult to know when and in which direction.”
  • No deviating views on the decision to keep rates on hold. “All members supported the proposal made by Mr Lane to keep the three key ECB interest rates unchanged.”

In short, even though the famous ‘good place’ is only mentioned twice in the minutes, it is clear that the ECB took an unchanged policy stance as the best option to tackle uncertainty. At the same time, the minutes seem to imply that the ECB’s willingness to react to a changing macro backdrop is higher than markets think.

Our base case sees the ECB staying on hold for the next two years, but...

Looking ahead, the bar for yet another rate cut from the European Central Bank remains high. In fact, the ECB currently feels very comfortable in what it calls a ‘good place.' With the ECB’s own growth forecasts seeing the eurozone economy growing by slightly more than 1% each year and inflation nicely settling down at 2% over the next few years, there is indeed very little reason to change its monetary policy stance.

At the same time, however, there are still some valid dovish arguments that could still force the central bank to cut further over the coming months. Just think of the delayed adverse impact of US tariffs, the stronger euro exchange rate, French politics or a delay in Germany’s fiscal stimulus. If any of these downside risks still materialise, we can see the ECB engaging in one or two more rate cuts. If not, and this is our base case scenario, interest rates will remain on hold for the next two years.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: EWT: Benefitting From The TSMC Surge, But Looks Overbought Now
data.description:
Introduction

The iShares MSCI Taiwan ETF (NYSEARCA:EWT) is a $6.75B-sized financial product that focuses on around 100 stocks from the Taiwanese stock market. EWT, which tracks, the MSCI Taiwan 25/50 Index, through the process of representative sampling, is setup to cover 85% of the Taiwanese stock market. However, because its tracking index is weighted on the basis of the free-float market cap of various stocks, this portfolio ends up tilting towards giant and large-cap names, which account for a whopping 93% of the total portfolio.

Nonetheless, EWT, which completed its silver jubilee on the markets in June this year, appears to have turned a corner in the second half of the current year, and is currently outperforming global stocks by over 600 bps on a YTD basis. Having said that, the performance is still not as resilient as other emerging markets, which are up by over 30% and outperforming EWT by over 500 bps.

Could EWT kick on even further, or are there other prospects in sight? Let’s dive into some of the key factors that are weighing on EWT’s performance.

Strong Linkage with US Tech Stocks And TSM

Prima facie, some of you may be tempted to believe that since EWT spreads its tentacles across 100 different Taiwanese stocks, there should ordinarily be a decent level of diversification across all these names. However, the reality is anything but that, with this product exposed to some serious idiosyncratic risks.

There are two key themes to flag here. The first is that just around one-fourth of the portfolio’s value comes from the stock of the semiconductor foundry giant- Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), and the second is that over two-thirds of the portfolio comes from technology stocks alone, making the linkage with US markets (which are also dominated by tech stocks) rather strong.

For further context, note that EWT’s 3-year beta with the S&P 500 works to 0.98x, which basically suggests that for every $1 movement in the US benchmark, EWT should typically almost keep pace, with an expected movement of $0.98. So, investors who typically look to EMs for some diversification away from tech heavy US stocks won’t quite get that with EWT.

TSM’s Price Appears to Be Getting Ahead of Its Fundamentals

Now coming back to TSM, it’s quite clear that it has been playing an instrumental role in EWT’s bounce back from the yearly lows, as the correlation of returns between the two has exceeded 0.8x since May and is now at 0.974x.

Investors may be interested to note that TSM is now at lifetime highs as it is seen as one of the fundamental cogs in the AI revolution, which has snowballed this year. The AI accelerators that are at the heart of modern data centers are all made by TSM’s HPC (High Performance Computing) platform, which is, by far, the largest platform of the latter, accounting for 60% of its total revenue.

So far so good, but investors may want to consider that when TSM publishes its Q3 results around a week from now, it is unlikely to be as strong as the Q2 results in July. In Q2, the topline had grown at an exceptional pace of 54%, but take a look at revenue growth expectations for not just Q3 (~33% YoY growth), but even the following 3 quarters (expected YoY growth of just around 4-20%). Even the margin profile (both at the gross and operating level) is expected to step down from what was seen in Q2 as the higher cost of operating overseas fabs leaves an adverse mark. Longer-term (over the next 5 years), EWT’s gross margins are expected to take a hit of 2.5% p.a. initially before this expands to 3.5% later on.

All in all, what we’re attempting to highlight is that TSM’s run-up is now largely sentiment-based, as the fundamentals going forward aren’t expected to be as resolute as what’s been seen in the recent past. When broader sections of the market start realizing this, it may affect TSM’s meteoric rise, and thus also weigh on EWT’s performance.

Exports Fuelling Taiwan’s GDP Performance

With regard to Taiwan’s own growth backdrop, things are certainly looking very healthy, with the Q2 GDP growth of 8% representing the best quarterly performance in 4 years!

Around 70% of Taiwan’s GDP comes from exports, and this played an integral role in Q2, surging by 35% YoY (Q1 growth was 19%). It looks like even export growth in Q3 too will be rather strong, as growth in July and August came in at 42% YoY and 38% YoY, respectively.

The still-resilient growth rate in August is quite striking, particularly when you consider that Taiwanese exports are now subject to a 20% tariff from the US. Admittedly, semiconductor exports from Taiwan are currently not “yet” subject to this, and that may help explain the resilience in exports. Although it remains to be seen how long this will carry on, 95% of US chip demand is being fulfilled by Taiwan.

What Do the Valuations of EWT Look Like?

Then, the last time we covered EWT, it was priced at a forward P/E of 15x (and offering superior long-term earnings growth of 21.2%, implying a rather compelling PEG of 0.71x), now the P/E ratio is even pricier at 16.5x (while the long-term earnings growth potential has come off by around 450 bps to 16.7%), which translates to an 18% premium over the corresponding multiple of a diversified emerging market stocks.

Closing Thoughts - What Do the Charts of EWT Suggest?

If you’re an investor who likes momentum plays, you’d be interested to note that EWT is in a good way, experiencing a series of higher-highs [HHs] and higher-lows [HLs] since April (this hasn’t stopped). Crucially, it is also trading above all its key daily moving averages (50, 100 and 200).

However, before you jump the gun, you may also want to take note of the larger time frame chart- the weekly chart, which suggests some caution may be warranted now. As you can see from the image below, the price is now on the cusp of hitting levels ($65-$69) which previously served as a stumbling block in 2021/2022.

In addition to that, also consider what the RSI indicator at the lower end of the chart is signaling. Basically, it points to overbought conditions, and the last time the RSI approached these levels in July 2024, the price of EWT had proceeded to correct by around 20%. We are not necessarily suggesting that one will see the same degree of correction, but we also don’t see great sense in trying to load up at these levels.

Separately, it appears that EM-focused investors won’t be incentivized or turned off by rotating into Taiwanese stocks now because EWT’s relative strength ratio (vs EMs) is basically on par with its long-term mean of around 1.2x.

To conclude, we reiterate our hold on EWT.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ULTA Beauty: The International Expansion Plan Is Taking Shape
data.description:
Short Summary of the Past Few Years

It's been a while since I've covered ULTA Beauty (NASDAQ:ULTA), but I've been following this company for a long time now. Back in 2023, I wrote about how ULTA was slowing down domestically in the USA, and that they needed to start looking beyond the USA. I would like to think that they've followed my advice, but the company has indeed started to expand internationally. These initiatives could bring back the growth for ULTA, which is what analysts are looking for.

The uncertainty around the company's long-term future started around 2023 and the stock has under-performed the market in 2024, as ULTA was slow in rolling out these plans for international expansion. However, after some delays, it seems that ULTA is now successfully expanding. As we expect that growth will start to pick up again, we rate the company as a hold due to its increased valuation, but improved growth prospects.

Three Initiatives (Mexico, Middle East, & UK)

At the moment, ULTA is rolling out their international expansion strategy. In summary they are focusing on three main markets: Mexico, the Middle East, and the UK. At the moment, the Mexican expansion is rolling out as planned with a joint venture, this is now starting to contribute to earnings this year.

In the middle east, ULTA has (finally) launched a franchise program, which could deliver high margins with relatively low risk. Franchise programs generally shift most of the risk to the franchisee, and do not require a large capital investment to the franchisor. They are also highly scalable, and could deliver strong growth. Moreover, the Middle East is an attractive market for premium beauty products due to increasing wealth, and partnering with a local operator is smart because they understand the market better.

Finally, ULTA acquired Space NK, operating in the UK and Ireland, expanding with around 80 stores. The UK is a big market that is relatively under-served, Sephora does not operate a lot of stores there yet relative to other markets.

Across these markets, ULTA is using different market entry strategies. I think that this is a good decision by ULTA's management, which is thoughtful in their approach. It seems to me like they are testing the waters and seeing what works. It might have taken them more time, but I feel that these plans are well prepared which is beneficial in the long-run than rushing them through.

Most Recent Earnings

The most recent earnings report from Q2 shows a very impressive 9.3% sales growth. This is driven by both improved comp sales, as well as the immediate impact of the Space NK acquisition, which was doing around 250m in revenue annually and grew 30% in 2024. This shows healthy performance from ULTA.

Capital Allocation

ULTA has a good sense of capital allocation, continuing its share repurchases, and investing back into the business. As some people might have expected, ULTA did not become a dividend stock tied to the US market, and the company still has a lot of potential to grow into a multinational beauty retailer.

FY 2025 and Long Term Outlook

ULTA updated its FY2025 outlook, and improved guidance during the Q2 conference call in August. The international growth plan is contributing to the top-line revenue, and the number of new stores openings is picking up again to 63 new stores this year. That is up from previous years with 30 and 47 new stores in FY2023 and FY2024, respectively. These improved growth prospects resulted in a higher valuation, the stock is now trading around a 23 times FWD P/E ratio, which could be reasonable but not cheap for a (strong) retailer.

Looking more long-term, I can provide some closing thoughts from this trends report from McKinsey. The beauty market is very healthy and has a long runway. Among other trends, male beauty is expanding in Gen Z and presents opportunity. This could double the market for ULTA beauty, which still caters to women mostly. However, we will have to see how this develops long-term.

Risks

The tariffs provide a risk for retailers importing goods from abroad. However, ULTA is relatively insulated from this risk as it does not directly import goods from China (only 1% of shipments). That being said, increased inflation would hurt discretionary consumption. We will have to see how this will develop.

As with every international expansion, the execution of the strategy could not succeed in all regions. ULTA is still in a testing phase across different markets. Having a strong balance sheet means that ULTA can make these investments and failure will not be catastrophic. The investments are relatively small and low risk, but they could provide substantial upside through improved growth.

Conclusions

While the last year was relatively disappointing, ULTA has beat expectations. My original thesis seems to be correct, in the sense that ULTA has managed to re-accelerate growth; starting its new chapter as an international company.

Last year provided a good buying opportunity at or below $400 per share, but the stock has risen again. Some of the upside has already been captured in the current stock price. That being said, ULTA still provides reasonable value at these prices for a strong company with good growth prospects. Long-term value investors who invested back in 2023 could take some profits.

data.title: Infosys Q2 Preview: Muted Expectations While Waiting For An AI Demand Wave
data.description:
In the run-up to the second quarter earnings season, Indian IT major's stock prices are pointing toward a muted outlook, with the Nifty IT index down 7-8% in the last 3 weeks. One of the major Indian IT companies, Infosys (NYSE:INFY), has seen a similar correction, as the company is set to report its 2QFY26 results on the 16th of October with muted expectations primarily driven by macroeconomic headwinds and recent geopolitical instability between India and the US.

I believe the IT services industry is currently at a pivotal juncture, which is marked by slower IT spending by enterprises and a potential AI-driven demand surge. This makes the next few quarters and commentaries regarding the end market coming out from the major IT players crucial, as this is when the market could turn around.

INFY, being a major IT player with significant investments in AI-related services, should be a potential major beneficiary of this AI demand surge. While the current stock price offers decent valuation, I believe waiting for minor signs of recovery would be beneficial before getting in the stock. Hence, I have a hold (neutral) rating for the time being.

2QFY26 preview: Muted growth and cautious optimism

INFY started the year on an upbeat note, with 1QFY26 beating muted expectations and management raising the lower end of the full-year revenue growth guidance. Back then there was some kind of uncertainty regarding the tariff issue that was causing delay in discretionary IT spending.

As the company transitioned into its second quarter (2QFY26), the acute tariff-related anxiety somewhat stabilized, but now new concerns regarding geopolitical instability are emerging. The fundamental client behavior has seen little change between the two quarters; the "wait and watch" approach continues, as highlighted by the Accenture (ACN) management in their recent earnings call.

Moreover, the ACN's management also retained a neutral stance while giving guidance by considering both macro deterioration and continuing a stable environment in macroeconomic conditions without pointing toward an improving outlook. I believe INFY's 2QFY26 commentary regarding the macro outlook should be in line with what Accenture has recently reported.

Given this backdrop of stability without significant improvement, I believe the 2QFY26 results will not present any meaningful deviations on either the topline or the margin front. Consequently, it is highly probable that INFY will maintain its full-year constant currency [CC] revenue growth guidance of 1% to 3%.

Geographically the European market should continue to perform well owing to recovery in spending from prior lows, while the US market should remain a drag on the overall company given the macroeconomic uncertainty leading the clients to hold on to discretionary spending.

While I do expect a muted outlook from INFY's earnings report, I would be keenly focused on management's commentary regarding subtle shifts in tone regarding potential recovery or any sort of positive developments.

Another important leading indicator to watch out for is the hiring trend, which has been quite muted for the last few quarters. While the management is committed to hiring 20k employees in FY26, the first quarter's lackluster hiring did point toward a cautious outlook by the management. Given the macro backdrop stabilizing now, one should keep an eye on if INFY keeps up with their provided guidance or not. Further, a ramp-up in hiring beyond initial guidance can point toward an improving market condition.

Potential AI spending wave could be around the corner

While things are tough now, INFY is trying to be a key beneficiary of the next big tech cycle driven by AI. The company has invested heavily in building its comprehensive AI suite (Infosys Topaz) and is continuously upskilling and reskilling its talent pool for complex AI-related projects.

While the current landscape is not yet ripe enough to reflect the true potential of AI-related demand, a future surge in AI spending is seen as inevitable. Historically, IT services spending has lagged behind the maturation of new product technologies. Clients tend to be skeptical of adopting a technology in its early stages when it is maturing rapidly, running the risk of their investments soon becoming obsolete if the underlying tech continues to deliver exponential improvements.

The floodgates of enterprise spending typically open once these exponential improvements convert into more predictable, incremental gains, as clients no longer run the risk of their investments becoming obsolete early and can realize the return on their IT spending.

I believe the recent slowdown in performance improvement from newer models like GPT-5 as compared to the huge leap taken by the older generation could be a sign that we are approaching a performance plateau. While performance gains can still be significant (like in the recent Claude Sonnet 4.5), they are not always the groundbreaking leaps seen in 2023-2024.

I believe that as more new-generation models with less dramatic performance gains are released, enterprises will become increasingly keen to deploy large-scale, durable AI-related investments, unlocking the next major wave of growth for which INFY is preparing.

Geopolitical Risk a major concern

The US is by far the largest market of INFY with over 60% of revenue coming from this single country. With the recent tussle between the Indian and US administrations over Russian crude oil purchases and trade deal negotiations, the Indian IT sector has started to witness some heat. In September, the US administration announced a $100k one-time fee for H1B visa applications aimed at creating a barrier for companies using foreign talent.

The Indian IT companies like INFY use this program to send local employees from India to the US for more frontend/client engagement roles. While the move would have limited impact on the company given the limited number of employees working in the US on H1B visas, just 2004 out of the total workforce of 323K employees.

While the H1B move is not a major risk for INFY, the primary concern is the protectionist approach of the US administration. The proposed Halting International Relocation of Employment (HIRE) act further confirms this protectionist stance with respect to foreign talents. This act aims to put a 25% tax on payments made by American companies for outsourced work, which is the primary business of IT services firms like INFY. While the act has a very slim chance of becoming a law given operational and political hurdles this protectionist stance could lead to more such initiatives by the current administration.

Valuation and conclusion

INFY currently trades at a 20.7x forward non-GAAP PE ratio, which is lower than the sector median of 25.3x and its own 5-year average of 26.7x. However, if we widen the horizon and take a decadal median, the company seems to be fairly valued.

While the company has major long-term opportunity, still the near-term uncertainty would keep the stock under pressure until signs of growth recovery emerge. Additionally, there are heightened geopolitical risks, and while recent steps would not have a material impact, the direction toward protectionism is a headwind for IT services businesses like INFY. Valuations do seem reasonable but I would wait for the situation to improve before issuing a buy rating. Until then, I have a neutral [HOLD] rating on the stock.

data.title: ARK Innovation ETF: Time To Go All In
data.description:
Here's a hot take for the day: buy a fund that, despite a poor five-year track record, down 9% cumulatively against the S&P 500's (SPY) 96% gains, has rallied to the stratosphere in the recent past. Oh, on a day that the ETF gained more than 3% in a single trading session.

This is my current position on ARK Innovation (BATS:ARKK). Cathie Wood's flagship ETF has climbed 111% in the past six months alone (see chart below). Such performance has not only put the high-flying Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) to shame, but it has also raised "overbought" red flags that have recently spooked most analysts on Seeking Alpha and other investors.

What I like most about ARKK

ARKK is exposed to the themes that I believe will create substantial value over the very long run, which I will define here as 20 years at least. These themes include electric and autonomous vehicles, represented by ARKK's largest holding, Tesla (TSLA); digital currency, expressed by Coinbase (COIN) at 6% portfolio allocation as of early October; and gaming and media, among others.

I continue to believe that, if given a very long investment horizon (say, my toddler's retirement portfolio), I would much rather buy and hold equity in the leaders of these technologies than low-valuation stocks in old-economy companies, including those in the O&G and industrial spaces, for example.

Yes, ARKK's top holdings are pricey, and have become much more so in the past half year. Among the top five holdings, which account for more than one-third of the portfolio's investments, three are rated F (the lowest rating) by Seeking Alpha on a forward P/E basis; and the other two, CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP) and Tempus AI (TEM), do not even have a projected positive EPS in the next couple of years to earn the right to have a P/E.

But to me, relative valuation does not matter as much in this case. By default, standard metrics like next-year P/E capture expected short-term performance, which does not match the long-term duration of ARKK's investments.

As a quick example, Shopify (SHOP), ARKK's sixth-largest holding, may trade at an aggressive 2025 EPS multiple of 112x today. But if the consensus is correct about long-term EPS growth rates at 30% (see below), the stock at its current price would trade at a P/E of only 8.2x in ten years. Either the company will eventually disappoint the market on bottom-line growth (certainly possible), or the share price will likely triple or more in the next decade to maintain a modest P/E of 25x in 2035, which would be much more reasonable for this stock.

What I dislike most about ARKK

If valuation (or recent share price outperformance) does not send me running away from ARKK, the fund's discretionary approach to investing makes me a bit hesitant. At a high level, the ETF focuses its investments on disruptive innovation. But how it executes the strategy is largely dependent upon ARK's managers and their team's quantitative and qualitative assessment.

The quasi-black box model impacts my ability to understand ARKK's future holdings, let alone estimate the performance of the fund. What makes it worse, in my view, is the fund manager's track record of selling high-flyers and buying dips, which effectively means that the relative losers remain (or gain prominence) in the portfolio, while the relative winners eventually take a back seat. I do not think that this is the best approach to investing in valuation-insensitive stocks like the ones that usually find their way into the ARKK portfolio (see my argument above on relative valuation).

Management fees, at a sizable 75 bps, could be cited as another weakness for this ETF. However, I do not think that less than one percent in drag to the ETF's annual returns will matter much in the long run, if or when it can perform substantially better (i.e., not by a few bps, but by a handful of percentage points) than the S&P 500.

How I would invest in ARKK today

If I had to rate ARKK from zero to ten on a fundamental criteria scale, I would probably choose a six: I am neither a fanboy nor a hater, although I lean in favor of investing in disruptive innovation.

What makes me much more bullish about owning ARKK today is the overlaying of technical markers, namely moving averages. ARKK has behaved like a bubble that inflated and then burst over the past ten years - and the ETF has finally started to recover again. I spoke at length about how to own and then dispose of potential bubble stocks and ETFs in the past, and ARKK has been one of the prime examples.

In my January 2022 article, I compared ARKK with the Nasdaq 100 during the dot-com event. I warned against owning ARKK at that time, the same way that owning the tech-rich index in December 2000, after an initial large dip that drew in bargain hunters, would have preceded a two-year streak of sizable, double-digit losses in 2001 and 2002.

To own a bubble confidently, I suggest selling the asset once the 50-day moving average dips below the 200-day moving average. This means that an investor can ride the bullish wave for as long as it lasts, but step aside when the tide begins to turn. Below is how this simple moving average strategy would have performed in the case of ARKK since the start of the decade: 19% annual returns vs. buy-and-hold's 12%, with a maximum drawdown of "only" 48% vs. buy-and-hold's 81% peak-to-trough decline.

Therefore, I think that now is a good time to own ARKK, despite the recent run. The ETF remains in a 40%-plus drawdown vs. peak 2021 levels, and it is still underwater over the past five years. The trick, however, is that I would monitor price action daily, as described above, to pull the plug on the investment should the recent doubling of the stock in a mere six months eventually prove to be ARKK's bubble 2.0.

data.title: HP: Stuck, But Still A Value Investor's Play
data.description:
Hewlett-Packard (NYSE:HPQ) remains a classic value investing opportunity these days, aimed for the long haul, highlighted by (1) high operational efficiency with consistently high returns on capital over the years; and (2) a relatively attractive valuation, reflecting a potentially not so well understood (in my opinion) defensive moat.

While in theory, HP is the kind of company that seems to do everything right behind the scenes, the problem is that it hasn't been enough when it needs to deliver to a market hungry for flash and hype. Slow, consistent, and predictable growth is naturally something largely ignored in bull market cycles. To make matters worse, around two-thirds of HP's profits still come from printing, a business that is more like a museum than a digital future.

So, in the short term, that weighs. In fact, based on historical returns, these factors have been weighted for quite some time. Growth investors look at HP and see little excitement with hopes of mild growth in a cyclical segment (PC), and a cash flow that looks tight and could potentially limit buybacks and dividends.

I wouldn't anticipate any short-term turnaround until HP signals to the market that it can truly grow outside of Printing, and generate consistent cash flow and allocate capital intelligently—whether with share buybacks, dividends, or new investments in PCs and corporate services.

However, trading at 8.6x forward P/E, practically in line with its historical average, I still think the HPQ thesis is quite de-risked for the long-term, and ticks many of the boxes of the value investing philosophy.

The proven resilience of HP's business tends to show its worth when the market cools. And so perhaps HPQ's main appeal today is not that it will explode in value during a crisis, but that it will probably suffer less than highly leveraged or hype-dependent growth companies, offering a kind of moderate hedge within the technology/electronics sector.

HP: A Model Long-Term Value Investment

Through the lens of value investing, Hewlett-Packard is a clear standout candidate for investors who prioritize names with high operational efficiency and attractive relative valuations.

To better demonstrate these attributes, Joel Greenblatt's methodology, which combines Return on Capital (ROC)—which basically measures the company's ability to generate profit from its invested capital—together with earnings yield (in the formula of operating profit "EBIT"/enterprise value), shows the investor's implicit return if the entire company were to be acquired.

As the table below shows, the ROC, calculated from EBIT divided by the sum of net working capital (NWC) plus net fixed assets (NFA), has consistently remained above 30% for most of the past decade. HP reached peak years between 2018 and 2021 (with an extremely high ROC >50%), and currently, in the last twelve months, an ROC of 28.3%, which is still solid, slightly lower than peak years.

In super-practical terms, an ROC above (or close to) 30% means that for every dollar HP invests in its business, it generates 30 cents in operating profit. And arguably, it makes much more sense for an investor to allocate their capital into HP, knowing that they can very likely turn it into a high percentage of profit. Although for some this may not be so impressive versus other high-growth tech peers, it is important to note the consistency here, as over the last decade, the average ROC would be 36%. Very few companies achieve this level of consistency, to be clear.

Furthermore, still using Greenblatt's methodology, there would be little point in this high return on capital in isolation if the investor's implicit return on the total price of the company were not attractive. This is why, using the Earnings Yield (EBIT/Enterprise Value) metric, it is possible to accurately measure HPQ valuations.

According to the chart below, over the last decade, HPQ has consistently maintained an EY of between 10% and 16%. If we consider that, based on current long-term bond rates, equity market risk premium, and adjusted beta, a cost of equity for HPQ today would be in the range of ~12.6%. Putting tax rate, debt/equity ratio, and cost of debt together, we could stipulate an after-tax WACC of ~10.2%.

As a rule of thumb, a return above the cost of capital invested implies the creation of real economic value for all financiers. Although the spread is relatively short between WACC and EY for HP, it can still be said that it continues to generate positive economic value, combining not only constant operational efficiency but also an attractive valuation.

As we can see over the last decade, precisely in periods when HP's ROC and EY were at their highest, its returns in the following years were much higher than the broader market.

Theoretically, companies with a very robust and consistent return on capital tend to grow sustainably over the long term.

And normally, companies that manage to do this consistently have a solid moat. In fact, I would dare to say that HP's moat is much more operational/defensive than technological. After all, the company has consolidated global operations in hardware, printers, PCs, and corporate services, which generates efficiencies of scale—something that allows for higher margins and consequently sustains a high ROC.

Probably because it doesn't have more of a technological moat, this is the best explanation for a high earnings yield over the past few years, with the market potentially not giving due credit to an ultra-efficient operating company.

But anyway, the moral of the story is simple: companies, like HP, that manage to reinvest profits with a high ROC should naturally perform better than companies with a low ROC.

HP in the Short-to-Mid Term

For short-term investors, key growth trends have recently dictated HP’s stock performance.

For example, in Fiscal Q3, HP saw a modest 3.1% YoY growth in revenues, slightly beating pessimistic expectations and virtually tied with CPI inflation.

The mix has been made up of tailwinds from the Personal Systems "PC division" (growing 6% YoY in revenues), mainly driven by replacement cycles of old PCs given the demand for AI-ready models, and headwinds from the Printing division, which is facing secular decline (falling 4% YoY in FQ3).

So, I would say here that HPQ has faced a mixed growth environment. But there are warning signs on the horizon. Two-thirds of operating profit (pro ROC basis) comes from the Printing segment, which is going in the complete opposite direction of automation and AI. There are also fears of slowing PC cycles with the current peak linked to the end of Windows 10 and a still reticent consumer spending going forward.

In the last two quarters of fiscal 2025, the impact of tariffs, mainly in the Personal Systems (PCs) segment, forced analysts to revise their 2025 EPS projections downwards by up to 12% from what was estimated six months ago. At similar levels, long-term guidance was revised downwards, capitalizing on further pain ahead.

In order to mitigate such headwinds, HP has accelerated the transfer of its manufacturing outside of China, seeking to establish manufacturing hubs in countries such as Vietnam, India, Mexico, and also in the U.S. The goal is to have more than 90% of the products sold in North America manufactured outside of China. In addition, HP is also seeking a restructuring that would involve cutting $300 million by the end of the current fiscal year (which ended in October), including cutting up to 2,000 jobs.

While layoffs are rarely something to be applauded, I believe these are strategies that demonstrate the necessary frugality at a time of adversity, which rightly brings HP back in line with its operating leverage and historically high returns on capital.

That said, HP comes relatively unimpressed to report its October quarter (for Fiscal Q4), expected to deliver $0.92 in EPS (down 0.8% YoY) and revenues of $14.4 billion (up 2.7% YoY).

I would keep my eyes open for free cash flow trends. In Fiscal Q3, HP showed a downward trend versus 2024, with the gap between FCF vs. operating cash flow widening, corroborating CapEx/margin pressure. With buybacks being cut, given the shorter financial slack, by year-to-date 2024, HP had returned $2.01 billion to shareholders, which was virtually all of its FCF.

In year-to-date 2025, it returned much less ($1.17 billion), suggesting that, although cash generation remains robust, much of the FCF has been used for structural maintenance rather than growth.

The continued high dependence on the Printing segment brings a greater risk of margin compression in the medium term. Therefore, if there is an expansion of EBIT margins, or if there is a smaller gap between OCF and FCF—eventually from a lighter or more efficient CapEx—a greater volume in buybacks would be very welcome and potentially decisive for the resumption of confidence in a short- to medium-term turnaround.

Risks and Final Remarks

HP doesn't have the burden of a high valuation to need to "overdeliver" like other tech names trading at explosive multiples do for a barely positive reaction. But neither does a low valuation signal that it can't go even lower.

To the extent that the Printing segment's dependence on the bottom line continues to be very relevant, the portent that margins will continue to be under pressure and in recession may be something that lasts for a long time, and not even very high and consistent returns on capital can seduce the market enough to generate returns above the benchmark.

On the other hand, arguably, we can say that, even with the slow growth, the pace has been at least enough to keep the operational/defensive moat still consistent for the long term, and the time to invest in value investing picks is precisely when the market is largely overlooking them.

For this reason, I believe that now may be a good time to own small amounts of HPQ, assuming that the horizon is based on the long term. It's difficult to time the market, or to know whether FQ4 or Fiscal 2026 will actually be the inflection point in the thesis. But with shares down ~15% year-to-date, punished by bearish reviews around negative sentiment in terms of consumer softness and tariffs inflating cost structure. Despite this, I remain confident that HP’s consistency could eventually generate gains for patient investors.
